subject: biology
ncdc_competency: The learner evaluates cells and tissues, by analysing and relating their structure to function, as a basis for medical research in order to improve health.

lessons.title: Chemicals of Life: Water and Lipids

content.body: All living cells are made of chemical compounds that perform specific roles. Water, lipids, proteins, and enzymes are the key chemicals that maintain cellular structure and drive metabolic processes.
Properties and Functions of Water

Water is the most abundant molecule in living cells (70–90% of cell content). Its properties arise from its polarity — the oxygen atom carries a slight negative charge and the hydrogen atoms carry slight positive charges, allowing hydrogen bonds to form between molecules.

content.heading: Key properties of water relevant to life:
items: High specific heat capacity: resists temperature changes — stabilises cell temperature.
High latent heat of vaporisation: evaporation removes large amounts of heat — cooling mechanism in sweating and transpiration.
Cohesion and adhesion: water molecules stick together (cohesion) and to other surfaces (adhesion) — supports water transport up xylem.
Universal solvent: polar nature allows ionic and polar substances to dissolve — enables chemical reactions in cells.
Metabolic role: water is a reactant in hydrolysis reactions and a product of condensation reactions.
Incompressibility: provides support in hydrostatic skeletons and turgidity in plant cells.

content.body: Note: Thermal properties of water (specific heat capacity and latent heat) are NOT required for examination in this syllabus.
Lipids: Simple and Conjugated
Lipids are non-polar, hydrophobic molecules that do not dissolve in water but dissolve in organic solvents. They are made mainly of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen.

content.heading: Simple lipids (triglycerides):
items: Made of one glycerol molecule joined to three fatty acid chains by ester bonds (formed in condensation reactions).
Saturated fatty acids: no double bonds between carbons — found mainly in animals, solid at room temperature.
Unsaturated fatty acids: one or more double bonds — found mainly in plants and fish, liquid at room temperature.
Functions: energy storage (2× more energy per gram than carbohydrates), thermal insulation, protection of organs.

content.heading: Conjugated lipids:
items: Phospholipids: glycerol + 2 fatty acids + phosphate group. The phosphate head is hydrophilic; the fatty acid tails are hydrophobic. This amphipathic nature causes phospholipids to form bilayers — the basis of all cell membranes.
Glycolipids: lipid + carbohydrate. Found on cell surface membranes — important in cell recognition.
Steroids: lipids with a four-ring carbon structure. Include cholesterol (membrane stability and hormone precursor), sex hormones, and vitamin D.

questions.question: A student investigates why water is used as a coolant in car radiators. Which property of water makes it suitable for this role?
questions.explanation: Water's high specific heat capacity means it absorbs large amounts of heat energy with only a small rise in temperature, making it an effective coolant.

questions.question: The cohesion of water molecules is due to:
options: Ionic bonds between hydrogen and oxygen
Hydrogen bonds between adjacent water molecules
Van der Waals forces between non-polar regions
questions.answer: Hydrogen bonds between adjacent water molecules
questions.explanation: The partial negative charge on oxygen and partial positive charge on hydrogen of adjacent molecules form hydrogen bonds, giving water its cohesive properties.

questions.question: A fatty acid with the formula CH₃(CH₂)₁₆COOH contains no double bonds. It is best described as:
questions.explanation: A fatty acid with no carbon-carbon double bonds is saturated — its carbon chain is fully 'saturated' with hydrogen atoms.

questions.question: The amphipathic nature of phospholipids means they:
options: Have both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions
Are only found in animal cells
questions.answer: Have both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions
questions.explanation: Phospholipids have a hydrophilic phosphate head and hydrophobic fatty acid tails. This causes them to arrange into bilayers in aqueous environments, forming cell membranes.

questions.question: Which lipid is a precursor for the synthesis of steroid hormones and vitamin D in the human body?
questions.explanation: Cholesterol is a steroid lipid found in cell membranes that also serves as a precursor molecule for the synthesis of steroid hormones such as oestrogen and testosterone, as well as vitamin D.

lessons.title: Chemicals of Life: Proteins and Enzymes

content.body: Protein Structure

Proteins are the most diverse biological molecules. They are polymers of amino acids joined by peptide bonds (formed in condensation reactions). There are 20 different amino acids, each with a unique R group (side chain).

content.heading: Levels of protein structure:
items: Primary structure: the sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain — determined by DNA.
Secondary structure: coiling (alpha helix) or folding (beta pleated sheet) due to hydrogen bonds between amino acids.
Tertiary structure: the overall 3D shape of the polypeptide — maintained by hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, disulphide bridges, and hydrophobic interactions.
Quaternary structure: two or more polypeptide chains bonded together (e.g. haemoglobin has 4 chains).

content.heading: Functional categories of proteins:
items: Structural proteins: collagen (connective tissue), keratin (hair/nails), elastin.
Carrier/transport proteins: haemoglobin (oxygen transport), channel proteins in membranes.
Enzymes: biological catalysts — speed up metabolic reactions.
Hormones: insulin, glucagon — chemical messengers.
Antibodies: immunoglobulins — immune defence.
Contractile proteins: actin and myosin — muscle movement.

content.body: Enzymes: Biological Catalysts
Enzymes are globular proteins that speed up chemical reactions without being consumed. They work by lowering the activation energy needed for a reaction to occur.

content.heading: Mechanisms of enzyme action:
items: Lock and Key model: the substrate fits precisely into the active site like a key into a lock. The active site shape is rigid and complementary to the substrate.

Induced Fit model (more accepted): the active site is flexible. When the substrate binds, the enzyme changes shape slightly to fit more closely around the substrate, increasing the chance of reaction.

content.body: Enzyme + Substrate → Enzyme-Substrate Complex → Enzyme + Products
Enzyme Inhibition

content.heading: Types of inhibition:
items: Competitive inhibition: an inhibitor molecule with a similar shape to the substrate competes for the active site. Increasing substrate concentration can overcome this. Example: malonate inhibits succinate dehydrogenase.

Non-competitive inhibition: the inhibitor binds to a different site on the enzyme (allosteric site), changing the shape of the active site so the substrate can no longer bind. Increasing substrate concentration cannot overcome this. Example: cyanide inhibits cytochrome oxidase.
End-product (feedback) inhibition: the final product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an early enzyme in the pathway — prevents overproduction.

content.title: Real-world application: Enzyme inhibition in medicine
content.body: Many medicines work as enzyme inhibitors. Statins inhibit an enzyme in cholesterol synthesis, reducing blood cholesterol levels. HIV drugs inhibit viral proteases, preventing viral replication.

questions.question: Haemoglobin is made of four polypeptide chains held together. This represents which level of protein structure?
questions.explanation: Quaternary structure involves two or more polypeptide chains bonded together. Haemoglobin has four chains (2 alpha, 2 beta), giving it quaternary structure.

questions.question: A student heats an enzyme solution to 80°C and finds it no longer works. The most likely reason is:
options: The enzyme ran out of substrate
The active site changed shape due to denaturation
The enzyme became a competitive inhibitor
questions.answer: The active site changed shape due to denaturation
questions.explanation: High temperatures break hydrogen bonds and other bonds maintaining tertiary structure, permanently changing the active site shape. The enzyme is denatured and can no longer bind its substrate.

questions.question: According to the Induced Fit model, when a substrate enters the active site:
options: The enzyme shape remains completely rigid
The enzyme changes shape slightly to fit the substrate more closely
The substrate changes its shape to fit the active site
questions.answer: The enzyme changes shape slightly to fit the substrate more closely
questions.explanation: The Induced Fit model proposes that the active site is flexible. Substrate binding induces a conformational change in the enzyme, creating a tighter fit that increases catalytic efficiency.

questions.question: Malonate has a similar structure to succinate and competes for the active site of succinate dehydrogenase. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Malonate is a classic example of a competitive inhibitor. It competes with succinate for the same active site. Increasing succinate concentration reduces malonate's effect.

questions.question: A drug binds to an enzyme at a site other than the active site, causing the active site to change shape so substrate can no longer bind. Adding more substrate has no effect. This inhibitor is:
questions.explanation: Non-competitive inhibitors bind to the allosteric site (not the active site), changing the enzyme's tertiary structure and distorting the active site. Increasing substrate concentration cannot overcome this because the inhibitor does not compete for the active site.

content.body: The Light Microscope

The light (optical) microscope uses visible light and a series of glass lenses to magnify specimens. It can magnify up to ×1500 and has a resolution of about 200 nm — meaning two points closer than 200 nm cannot be distinguished as separate.

content.heading: Key terms:
items: Magnification: how many times larger the image is compared to the actual object.
Resolution: the ability to distinguish two adjacent points as separate — determines clarity of image.
Total magnification = eyepiece lens × objective lens (e.g. 10× × 40× = 400×)

content.body:
Magnification = Image size ÷ Actual size

Actual size = Image size ÷ Magnification

Example: Image of cell = 30 mm, magnification = ×300
Actual size = 30 mm ÷ 300 = 0.1 mm = 100 µm
Preparing a Slide

content.heading: Steps for a temporary wet mount slide:
items: Place a small piece of tissue on a clean glass slide.
Add a drop of water or stain (e.g. iodine for starch, methylene blue for nuclei).
Lower a coverslip gently at an angle to avoid air bubbles.
Observe first under low power to find the specimen, then increase magnification.

content.body: Measuring Cells: Field of View

The field of view is the circular area visible through the microscope. To estimate the actual size of a cell, measure the diameter of the field of view using a stage micrometer, then estimate how many cells fit across the field.

Estimated cell diameter = Field of view diameter ÷ Number of cells across the field

Units: 1 mm = 1000 µm (micrometres)
         1 µm = 1000 nm (nanometres)
Electron Microscopes

content.heading: Types and comparison:
items: Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM): electrons pass through a thin section of specimen. Shows internal ultrastructure. Magnification up to ×500,000. Resolution ~0.1 nm.
Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM): electrons scan the surface of a specimen. Produces 3D images. Useful for studying cell surfaces and organelle shapes.
Both use electrons (not light), so specimens must be in a vacuum — only dead material can be viewed.

questions.question: A cell appears 24 mm wide in a micrograph taken at a magnification of ×600. What is the actual width of the cell?
questions.explanation: Actual size = Image size ÷ Magnification = 24 mm ÷ 600 = 0.04 mm = 40 µm.

questions.question: Resolution in microscopy refers to:
options: How much larger the image appears
The ability to distinguish two adjacent points as separate
questions.answer: The ability to distinguish two adjacent points as separate
questions.explanation: Resolution determines the clarity of detail. A higher resolution means finer details can be distinguished. The electron microscope has much greater resolution than the light microscope.

questions.question: A student wants to examine the 3D surface texture of a pollen grain. Which microscope is most suitable?
questions.explanation: SEM scans the surface of specimens and produces 3D images, making it ideal for studying surface features such as pollen grain texture.

questions.question: Why can only dead specimens be examined using an electron microscope?
options: Specimens must be placed in a vacuum, which is incompatible with life
The magnification is too high for living cells
Staining for electron microscopy is toxic
questions.answer: Specimens must be placed in a vacuum, which is incompatible with life
questions.explanation: Electron microscopes require a vacuum so that electrons are not scattered by air molecules. Living organisms cannot survive in a vacuum.

questions.question: A microscope has an eyepiece of ×10 and objective lenses of ×4, ×10, and ×40. What is the highest total magnification available?
questions.explanation: Total magnification = eyepiece × objective = 10 × 40 = ×400.

lessons.title: Ultrastructure of Animal, Plant and Bacterial Cells

content.body: Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic Cells
All cells are either prokaryotic or eukaryotic. This is the most fundamental division in biology.

content.heading: Prokaryotic cells (bacteria):
items: No membrane-bound nucleus — DNA is a circular chromosome in the nucleoid region.
No membrane-bound organelles (no mitochondria, ER, Golgi).
Smaller ribosomes (70S, made of 30S + 50S subunits).
Cell wall made of peptidoglycan (murein).
May have: plasmids (small circular DNA), flagella, pili, capsule.
Average size: 1–10 µm.

content.heading: Eukaryotic cells (plants and animals):
items: Membrane-bound nucleus containing linear chromosomes.
Membrane-bound organelles: mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus.
Larger ribosomes (80S, made of 40S + 60S subunits).
Average size: 10–100 µm.

content.body: Animal Cell Organelles and Functions

items: Nucleus: contains DNA (chromosomes), controls cell activities, has a double membrane (nuclear envelope) with nuclear pores.
Nucleolus: within the nucleus — produces ribosomal RNA (rRNA).
Mitochondria: double membrane organelle — site of aerobic respiration (ATP production). Inner membrane folded into cristae to increase surface area.
Ribosomes (80S): site of protein synthesis — found free in cytoplasm or on rough ER.
Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (RER): studded with ribosomes — synthesises and transports proteins.
Smooth Endoplasmic Reticulum (SER): no ribosomes — synthesises lipids and steroids, detoxifies drugs.
Golgi apparatus: stack of flattened membranes — modifies, packages, and secretes proteins and lipids.
Lysosomes: contain digestive enzymes — break down old organelles, bacteria, and worn-out cell parts.
Centrioles: organise spindle fibres during cell division — found only in animal cells.
Plasma membrane: controls what enters and leaves the cell.

content.body: Additional Structures in Plant Cells

items: Cell wall: made of cellulose — rigid, gives structural support, freely permeable.
Chloroplasts: site of photosynthesis — contain thylakoids (grana) and stroma. Double membrane.
Large central vacuole: filled with cell sap — maintains turgor pressure.
Plasmodesmata: cytoplasmic connections through cell walls — allow communication between cells.
No centrioles, no lysosomes (in most plant cells).

content.body: The Fluid Mosaic Model of the Plasma Membrane
Proposed by Singer and Nicolson (1972). The membrane is described as a phospholipid bilayer with proteins embedded in or floating on it — like a mosaic of tiles in a fluid.

content.heading: Components and their functions:
items: Phospholipid bilayer: hydrophilic heads face outward (toward water), hydrophobic tails face inward — selectively permeable barrier.
Integral (intrinsic) proteins: span the whole membrane — include channel proteins (allow specific ions/molecules through) and carrier proteins.
Peripheral (extrinsic) proteins: on the membrane surface — involved in cell signalling and recognition.
Glycoproteins: proteins with carbohydrate chains — cell recognition, receptor sites for hormones.
Glycolipids: lipids with carbohydrate chains — cell recognition.
Cholesterol: between phospholipids — regulates membrane fluidity (prevents it being too rigid in cold or too fluid in heat).

questions.question: A bacterium and a liver cell are both examined under an electron microscope. Which feature would be present in the liver cell but NOT in the bacterium?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are membrane-bound organelles found only in eukaryotic cells. Bacteria (prokaryotes) lack membrane-bound organelles. Both cells have a membrane, ribosomes (different sizes), and DNA.

questions.question: A protein is synthesised, modified, and secreted from a cell. In the correct order, which organelles are involved?
questions.explanation: Proteins are synthesised by ribosomes on the RER. They travel through the RER to the Golgi apparatus where they are modified and packaged into vesicles for secretion.

questions.question: Cholesterol is found in the plasma membrane. Its main role is to:
options: Act as a receptor site for insulin
questions.explanation: Cholesterol molecules sit between phospholipids. In cold temperatures, they prevent the membrane becoming too rigid by disrupting the packing of tails. In warm temperatures, they reduce excessive fluidity.

questions.question: A plant cell is placed in a concentrated salt solution. Its central vacuole shrinks. This is because water leaves the vacuole by:
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water from a region of high water potential (dilute solution inside the vacuole) to a region of low water potential (concentrated salt solution outside) through a selectively permeable membrane.

questions.question: The cell wall of a bacterium is chemically different from the cell wall of a plant. The bacterial cell wall is made of:
questions.explanation: Bacterial cell walls are made of peptidoglycan (also called murein), a polymer of sugars and amino acids. Plant cell walls are made of cellulose. This difference is exploited by antibiotics like penicillin, which disrupt peptidoglycan synthesis.

lessons.title: Diversity of Plant and Animal Tissues

content.body: A tissue is a group of cells with similar structure that perform a specific function. Tissues are organised into organs, which form organ systems. Understanding tissue structure helps in disease diagnosis and medical research.
Plant Tissues

items: Parenchyma: thin cellulose cell walls, large central vacuole, loosely packed. Found throughout the plant. Functions: photosynthesis (if contains chloroplasts), storage of starch and water, packing tissue.

Collenchyma: unevenly thickened cellulose cell walls (corners thickened), living cells. Found beneath the epidermis of stems and leaves. Function: flexible mechanical support — allows bending without breaking.

Sclerenchyma: thick, lignified (waterproof, rigid) cell walls. Cells die at maturity. Two types: fibres (long, pointed cells — e.g. flax) and sclereids (short, hard cells — e.g. seed coats). Function: rigid mechanical support.

Xylem: thick lignified walls, dead cells forming hollow tubes. Vessels (wide, no end walls) and tracheids (narrow, tapered). Functions: transport of water and mineral ions up the plant, mechanical support.

Phloem: living cells. Sieve tube elements (have sieve plates, no nucleus) + companion cells (have nucleus, control sieve tube). Function: transport of sucrose and amino acids (assimilates) up and down the plant (translocation).

content.body: Animal Tissues

items: Epithelial tissue: sheets of tightly packed cells covering body surfaces and lining organs. Types: squamous (flat, for diffusion — e.g. alveoli, blood capillaries), cuboidal (cube-shaped — kidneys), columnar (tall — intestine lining, often with microvilli for absorption), ciliated (with cilia — trachea, sweeps mucus).

Cardiac muscle tissue: branched, striated muscle cells with intercalated discs (allow electrical signals to pass between cells). Found only in the heart. Property: myogenic — can contract rhythmically without nervous stimulation.

Areolar connective tissue: loose arrangement of fibres (collagen and elastin) in a matrix with scattered cells (fibroblasts, macrophages, mast cells). Found beneath skin, surrounding organs. Function: packing tissue, supports and connects other tissues.
Fibrous connective tissue (dense): densely packed collagen fibres. Found in tendons (muscle to bone) and ligaments (bone to bone). Function: strong, flexible connection.
Skeletal muscle tissue: long, cylindrical, multinucleate, striated cells (muscle fibres). Voluntary control. Function: movement and locomotion.

content.title: Tissues and disease diagnosis
content.body: A biopsy involves removing a small tissue sample from the body and examining it under a microscope. Abnormal cell division or changed cell structure indicates cancer. For example, examining cervical tissue cells (cervical smear) can detect early signs of cervical cancer before symptoms appear — this saves lives through early treatment.
Levels of Organisation

items: Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ System → Organism
Each level of organisation allows greater specialisation and efficiency.
Example: cardiac muscle cells form heart muscle tissue → forms the heart organ → part of the circulatory system.

questions.question: A botanist examines a stem cross-section and observes cells with unevenly thickened cellulose walls at the corners. These cells are still alive. The tissue is:
questions.explanation: Collenchyma cells have unevenly thickened cellulose walls, especially at the corners, and remain living at maturity. They provide flexible support in young, growing plant parts.

questions.question: Xylem vessels are well adapted for water transport because they:
options: Are living cells with many mitochondria
Are dead, hollow tubes with lignified walls and no end walls
Contain companion cells to control flow
Have sieve plates to regulate transport
questions.answer: Are dead, hollow tubes with lignified walls and no end walls
questions.explanation: Xylem vessels are formed from dead cells. The end walls break down, forming continuous hollow tubes. Lignin waterproofs the walls and prevents collapse under the tension of water transport.

questions.question: A patient is diagnosed with a blocked coronary artery. An important property of cardiac muscle is that it is myogenic. This means:
options: It contracts only when stimulated by the nervous system
It can contract rhythmically without external nerve stimulation
It contains more mitochondria than skeletal muscle
questions.answer: It can contract rhythmically without external nerve stimulation
questions.explanation: Myogenic means the heart muscle generates its own electrical impulses from within (the sinoatrial node). This allows the heart to beat continuously without requiring constant nerve signals.

questions.question: Phloem transport (translocation) differs from xylem transport because:
options: Phloem transports only in an upward direction
Phloem is made of dead cells only
Phloem transports organic solutes in both directions
questions.answer: Phloem transports organic solutes in both directions
questions.explanation: Phloem translocates sucrose and amino acids from sources (leaves) to sinks (roots, fruits, growing tips) — movement can be upward or downward depending on where the sink is. Xylem only transports upward.

questions.question: A doctor takes a biopsy from a patient's liver and observes cells with abnormally large nuclei and irregular shapes dividing rapidly. This most likely indicates:
questions.explanation: Cancer cells are characterised by abnormal size, irregular shape, large nuclei, and uncontrolled rapid division. A biopsy revealing these features indicates malignancy and would require further investigation and treatment.
